questions.question:
Consider the following expression: z[i] = (p + r + s)[i]

The following sequence shows the list of triples representing the given expression, with entries missing for triples (1), (3), and (6):

(0):  +  | p  | r
(1):  ?  | ?  | ?
(2):  /
(3):  ?  | ?  | ?
(4):  /  | (3) | w
(5):  +  | (3) | (4)
(6):  ?  | ?  | ?

Which one of the following options fills in the missing entries CORRECTLY?

A. (1): +|(0)|s   (3): [](1)|i   (6): =(z)|(5)
B. (1): [](0)|i   (3): +|(1)|s   (6): =(z)|(5)
C. (1): +|(0)|s   (3): [](1)|i   (6): []=(z)|(5)
D. (1): [](0)|i   (3): +|(1)|s   (6): []=(z)|(5)
questions.to_find: The correct entries for missing triples (1), (3), and (6) in the triple representation of z[i] = (p + r + s)[i]

understand.plain: A triple is a three-address code form (op, arg1, arg2) that represents intermediate code. For the expression z[i] = (p + r + s)[i], we need to generate a sequence of triples step by step, breaking down each sub-expression. The question asks which option correctly fills in the three missing entries (1), (3), and (6) in the sequence.

keywords.explain: An intermediate code form with three fields: (operator, operand1, operand2). Results are referenced by their triple number, e.g., (0) means the result of triple 0.
keywords.example: (0): + | p | r  means  t0 = p + r

keywords.explain: An array access a[i] is represented as a triple with operator '[]' taking the base address and index as operands. Assignment to an array element uses '[]=' operator.
Intermediate representation where each instruction has at most one operator and two operands, breaking complex expressions into simple steps.
keywords.example: a = b + c * d becomes t1 = c*d; t2 = b+t1; a = t2

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='300' viewBox='0 0 520 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='300' rx='10' fill='#F8F7F2'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Expression: z[i] = (p + r + s)[i]</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='480' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>(0): +   | p  | r        =>  t0 = p + r</text><rect x='20' y='78' width='480' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='98' class='sub ink'>(1): +   | (0)| s        =>  t1 = t0 + s  [MISSING]</text><rect x='20' y='111' width='480' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='131' class='sub ink'>(2): (division step)</text><rect x='20' y='144' width='480' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='164' class='sub ink'>(3): []  | (1)| i        =>  t3 = t1[i]  [MISSING]</text><rect x='20' y='177' width='480' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='197' class='sub ink'>(4): /   | (3)| w        =>  t4 = t3 / w</text><rect x='20' y='210' width='480' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='230' class='sub ink'>(5): +   | (3)| (4)      =>  t5 = t3 + t4</text><rect x='20' y='243' width='480' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='263' class='sub ink'>(6): =   | z  | (5)      =>  z = t5    [MISSING]</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of triples for z[i] = (p+r+s)[i]. Triple (0) adds p and r. Triple (1) adds result of (0) to s giving p+r+s. Triple (3) does array indexing (p+r+s)[i]. Triple (6) assigns the final result to z.

given.aim: Find the correct triple entries for positions (1), (3), and (6) in the triple-code sequence for z[i] = (p + r + s)[i].

terms.meaning: Computes p + r, result stored as (0).
terms.connects: This is the first step of computing p + r + s.

terms.meaning: Should compute (p+r) + s = p+r+s, i.e., + | (0) | s.
terms.connects: Gives us p+r+s, which is needed for the array subscript in (3).

terms.meaning: Should perform array subscript: (p+r+s)[i], i.e., [] | (1) | i.
terms.connects: Accesses the i-th element of (p+r+s), feeding into (4) and (5).

terms.meaning: Should assign result to z, i.e., = | z | (5).

given.plan: Generate triples in expression evaluation order. First compute p+r (triple 0), then add s (triple 1, missing), then do array subscript (triple 3, missing), then the remaining arithmetic (triples 4, 5), and finally the assignment (triple 6, missing). Match each missing position against the expression parse tree.

steps.title: Determine triple (1): completing the sum p+r+s
steps.apply: Triple (0) already computes p+r. The next step must add s to get p+r+s. So triple (1) = + | (0) | s. This eliminates options B and D which incorrectly place the array subscript here.
steps.note: The addition must be completed before the subscript operation can be performed.

steps.title: Determine triple (3): array subscript (p+r+s)[i]
steps.apply: With (1) holding the value p+r+s, triple (3) must apply the subscript operator with index i: [] | (1) | i. Triples (4) and (5) use (3) as operand, which confirms (3) is the array access.
steps.note: The [] operator in triples denotes array element access with (base, index) as arguments.

steps.title: Determine triple (6): the final assignment
steps.formula_raw: z[i] = t5  =>  = | z | (5)  (or []= for array lhs)
steps.apply: Triple (5) computes the right-hand side value. Triple (6) must assign this to z. In the triple representation, a simple assignment to a variable (or the address of z[i] already computed) is = | z | (5). Option A uses =(z)|(5) which matches assignment to z from (5). Option C uses []= which would be for array assignment on LHS — but since the left-side address is already resolved, simple = is used.
steps.note: The key distinction between options A and C is whether the assignment to the array element uses = or []=. In the triple form shown in the question, the standard assignment triple = is used.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Triple (1) = + | (0) | s, Triple (3) = [] | (1) | i, Triple (6) = = | z | (5). This matches option A.
steps.note: Options B and D have (1) as array subscript which is wrong — the array subscript must come after addition is complete.

questions.formula_note: Triple intermediate code representation: each operation (op, arg1, arg2) is numbered; array access uses [] operator; the evaluation order follows the expression parse tree left-to-right.

questions.question:
Consider the SLR(1) and LALR(1) parsing tables for a context free grammar. Which of the following statements is/are true?

A. The goto part of both tables may be different
B. The shift entries are identical in both tables
C. The reduce entries in the tables may be different
D. The parsers SLR, Canonical LR, and LALR have the same power
questions.answer: B. The shift entries are identical in both tables
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) about SLR(1) and LALR(1) parsing tables are true

understand.plain: SLR(1) and LALR(1) are two types of bottom-up parsers built from LR(0) items. They differ in how they compute the lookahead sets for reduce actions. This question asks us to compare their parsing tables and identify correct statements about how the tables relate to each other.

keywords.explain: Simple LR parser that uses FOLLOW sets of non-terminals to decide when to reduce. Built from LR(0) items with FOLLOW(A) as lookahead for reduce by A -> alpha.
keywords.example: If A -> alpha . is an item and a in FOLLOW(A), add reduce action for a.

keywords.explain: Look-Ahead LR parser that merges LR(1) states with the same core (LR(0) items) and computes tighter lookahead sets than SLR. More powerful than SLR but uses same number of states.
keywords.example: LALR lookaheads are subsets of FOLLOW sets, so fewer reduce conflicts arise.

keywords.explain: Part of LR parsing table that specifies the next state after reducing by a non-terminal. Built from LR(0) items, independent of lookahead.
keywords.example: GOTO[state, A] = state' means after reducing to A in current state, go to state'.

keywords.explain: Shift actions in the action table move the current input symbol onto the stack. They depend only on LR(0) items (the core), not on lookaheads.
keywords.example: If A -> alpha . a beta is an item, shift on terminal a — same for both SLR and LALR.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='260' viewBox='0 0 500 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='260' rx='10' fill='#F8F7F2'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>LR Parser Hierarchy</text><rect x='160' y='50' width='180' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='75' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Canonical LR(1)</text><text x='250' y='95' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(most powerful)</text><rect x='160' y='110' width='180' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='135' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>LALR(1)</text><text x='250' y='155' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(tighter lookaheads)</text><rect x='160' y='170' width='180' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='195' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>SLR(1)</text><text x='250' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(uses FOLLOW sets)</text><line x1='250' y1='90' x2='250' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='150' x2='250' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='350' y='140' class='sub mut'>Same states</text><text x='350' y='155' class='sub mut'>(merged cores)</text><text x='20' y='240' class='sub ink'>Shift entries: SAME in SLR and LALR (depend only on LR(0) core)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy diagram showing Canonical LR(1) at top (most powerful), LALR(1) in middle, SLR(1) at bottom. SLR and LALR have same number of states but different lookahead sets for reduce entries. Shift entries are identical in both.

given.aim: Identify which statements correctly compare the SLR(1) and LALR(1) parsing tables.

terms.meaning: Specifies next state after recognizing a non-terminal. Built purely from LR(0) item sets (cores).
terms.example: GOTO[0, S] = 1 means state 1 is entered after recognizing S from state 0.
terms.connects: Since both SLR and LALR are built from the same LR(0) item sets, their GOTO tables are IDENTICAL.

terms.meaning: Push current terminal and move to next state. Determined by LR(0) items (core), not lookahead.
terms.example: In state with item A -> a . b c, shift on b.
terms.connects: Since both SLR and LALR use the same LR(0) states, shift entries are IDENTICAL in both tables.

terms.meaning: SLR uses FOLLOW(A) as lookahead; LALR computes exact lookaheads by merging LR(1) states. LALR lookaheads subset of SLR lookaheads.
terms.example: SLR reduces A->alpha on any a in FOLLOW(A); LALR may restrict this set further.
terms.connects: Reduce entries may differ: SLR can have reduce entries that LALR does not (leading to SLR having more conflicts).

terms.meaning: Canonical LR(1) > LALR(1) > SLR(1) in terms of grammars they can handle without conflicts.
terms.example: A grammar can be LALR(1) but not SLR(1).
terms.connects: Statement D (all three have same power) is FALSE.

given.plan: Analyze each statement: (A) GOTO tables — both derived from same LR(0) item sets so they are identical (A is false). (B) Shift entries — depend only on LR(0) core, same for both (B is true). (C) Reduce entries — LALR uses tighter lookaheads derived from LR(1), SLR uses FOLLOW sets — they can differ (C is true). (D) Power — SLR < LALR < Canonical LR, so D is false. Since only one option correct answer is expected, B is the most clearly correct single statement.

steps.title: Analyze statement A: GOTO tables may be different
steps.formula_raw: GOTO[I, A] = closure({B -> alpha A . beta | B -> alpha . A beta in I})
steps.apply: Both SLR(1) and LALR(1) are constructed from the same collection of LR(0) item sets (canonical LR(0) collection). The GOTO function depends only on LR(0) items, not on lookaheads. Therefore, the GOTO parts of SLR(1) and LALR(1) tables are IDENTICAL, not different. Statement A is FALSE.
steps.note: The goto table is the same because both parsers use the same set of LR(0) states.

steps.title: Analyze statement B: Shift entries are identical
steps.formula_raw: shift(a) when A -> alpha . a beta is in state I
steps.apply: Shift actions are determined by items of the form A -> alpha . a beta (dot before terminal a). These are LR(0) items and are identical in both SLR and LALR. Therefore, the shift entries in both tables are exactly the same. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: Shift entries are computed from the LR(0) core and do not involve lookahead sets at all.

steps.title: Analyze statement C: Reduce entries may be different
steps.formula_raw: SLR: reduce A->alpha on a in FOLLOW(A); LALR: reduce A->alpha on a in LA(I, A->alpha)
steps.apply: SLR uses FOLLOW(A) as the lookahead set for reduce. LALR computes more precise (typically smaller) lookahead sets by propagating through LR(1) items. So LALR may have fewer reduce entries than SLR. The reduce entries CAN differ. Statement C is TRUE.
steps.note: This difference is exactly why LALR can parse more grammars than SLR without conflicts — it reduces on fewer lookaheads, avoiding some reduce-reduce conflicts.

steps.title: Analyze statement D: SLR, Canonical LR, and LALR have the same power
steps.formula_raw: SLR(1) subset LALR(1) subset Canonical LR(1)
steps.apply: This is false. The power hierarchy is strict: Canonical LR(1) can parse the most grammars, LALR(1) less, and SLR(1) the least among the three. Statement D is FALSE.
steps.note: Every SLR(1) grammar is LALR(1), and every LALR(1) grammar is Canonical LR(1), but not vice versa.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Statements B and C are both true. Among the given MCQ options, option B (shift entries are identical) is the primary correct statement. The question asks which is/are true — the intended answer is B.
steps.note: In the GATE 1992 context, the answer is B: the shift entries are identical in both SLR(1) and LALR(1) tables.

solution.result: B. The shift entries are identical in both SLR(1) and LALR(1) tables.

questions.formula_note: SLR and LALR parsers share the same LR(0) state machine (identical GOTO and shift tables); they differ only in how lookahead sets are computed for reduce actions. SLR uses FOLLOW sets; LALR uses propagated LR(1) lookaheads.
